figures.caption: Figure 2: ICL performance of SpiritLM given demonstrations synthesized by KokoroTTS. (A): performance on the Core condition across 1-5 demonstrations; (B): performance on the Core condition, separated by the syntactic structure of the target and the demonstration; (C): performance under various types of linguistic overlap between demonstration and target; (D): performance under various acoustic manipulations of the demonstrations. Shaded regions/error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals.

Figure 3: Acoustic features of SpiritLM speech outputs given different demonstration types. Top row: demonstrations are synthesized using KokoroTTS; Bottom row: demonstrations are synthesized using SpeechT5. Shaded regions indicate 95% confidence intervals.
Figure 8: Attention to prefix-matching and non-prefix-matching tokens (speech vs. text) by different head groups, across three different demonstration types.

Figure 7: Prefix-matching scores for each head in SpiritLM on sequences of random tokens (text tokens only, speech tokens only, or interleaved text and speech tokens). The results of Llama-2-7B (the text-only backbone of SpiritLM) are added as a reference.

Figure 1: Our experimental setup: we construct ICL prompts and apply different linguistic and acoustic manipulations. We then (A) evaluate SpiritLM’s performance on the TTS task under each manipulation, and (B) measure the acoustic characteristics of the output speech.
Figure 5: Word Error Rate of different Whisper model sizes on various speech types.

Figure 6: ICL performance of SpiritLM given demonstrations synthesized by SpeechT5. (A): performance on the Core condition across 1-5 demonstrations; (B): performance on the Core condition, separated by the syntactic structure of the target and the demonstration; (C): performance under various types of linguistic overlap between demonstration and target; (D): performance under various acoustic manipulations of the demonstrations. Shaded regions/error bars indicate 95% confidence intervals.

Table 1: Illustrative examples of linguistic overlap conditions. Examples are shown for sentences with active syntax; the same conditions apply to the passive sentences. The overlapping linguistic elements between the demonstration and target sentences are underlined. Note that verb tense deliberately only agrees between demonstration and target in the function words only and all words condition.
Figure 4: Identifying and ablating induction heads in SpiritLM.
